title: Axiomatic Characterization of the Hamming and Jaccard Distances
abstract: Measures of dissimilarity between a pair of objects can play a pivotal role in many machine learning objectives such as clustering, outlier detection, or data visualization. In this paper, we focus on data in the form of binary vectors and analyze several methods of measuring dissimilarity between them. We introduce several properties, *axioms*, that a measure of dissimilarity can satisfy and characterize the *Hamming* and *Jaccard* distances as the only measures satisfying particular subsets of our axioms. Based on our analysis, we identify shortcomings of both distances and propose novel approaches that are better suited for certain applications. We complement our theoretical findings by an extensive empirical study. Our primary motivation is the analysis of election data, in which the votes have the form of binary approval of alternatives, but the applicability of our results reaches far beyond that.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper studies the axiomatic characterization of distance metrics for binary vectors, represented by the Hamming and Jaccard distances. The Hamming distance is minimally characterized by Anonymity, Scaling, Independence Symmetry, 0-1 Symmetry, Convergence, Triangle Inequality, and Normalization, while the Jaccard distance is by the same set of axioms except for 0-1 Symmetry being replaced with Add-Zero. This is the first contribution.

Moreover, a pitfall of the axiom Independence Symmetry is pointed out, which is inappropriate when we want to assess the inclusion relationship between two binary vectors. By replacing the arithmetic mean included in the Hamming and Jaccard distances with the geometric mean (and its generalization), the authors propose a p-Hamming and p-Jaccard distances.

In the experiments, the authors investigate (1) how distance metrics well align 1-D and 2-D geometry (2) whether distance metrics yield a visually reasonable cluster structures and (3) whether distance metrics are amenable to real-world label structures. To this end, the geometric Jaccard distance (p=0) looks work well in all scenarios. This fact validates the benefit to deviate from Independence Symmetry.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
**Strengths**

This paper is well-motivated and written nicely. Usually the principled design of distance metrics is not straightforward, and I believe this paper paves a way towards it. We can have a better understanding of what properties these common distances like Hamming and Jaccard do entail. These are good enough reasons for accepting this paper.

**Weaknesses**

Basically I don't think this paper has significant flaws and weaknesses. Just for suggestions, I'd like to raise a few points.

+ Can you discuss whether generalized p-Hamming and p-Jaccard distances preserve the other axioms than Independence Symmetry?
+ Discussing other popular distances such as Dice index, Tversky index, and Sympson's overlap coefficient looks nice. Do they satisfy any of the axioms in Table 1? This comparison should give us better highlights on different metrics.

Very minor points:

+ l227: The first "d" in "d-dimensional" should be italicized.
+ l238: The symbol "d" conflicts with the dimension.

QUESTIONS:
See "Strengths and weaknesses".

LIMITATIONS:
This work may not be subject to negative societal impacts due to its theoretical nature.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
In this paper, the authors show the axiomatic characterization of the Hamming and Jaccard distances. Their results are as follows:
(1) Hamming distance satisfies anonymization, scaling, independent symmetry, 0-1 symmetry, convergence, triangle inequality, normalization, but not add 0.
(2) Hamming distance is the only distance that satisfies anonymization, scaling, independent symmetry, 0-1 symmetry, convergence, triangle inequality, normalization.
(3) Jaccard distance satisfies anonymization, scaling, independent symmetry, convergence, triangle inequality, normalization, and add 0. The distance does not satisfy 0-1 symmetry.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
I recommend a rejection of this paper based on the following reasons:
A. As acknowledged by the authors themselves at Line 122, Contribution (1) is trivial and does not provide a substantial advance.

B. While Contributions (1) and (3) have some relevance to machine learning, I find the motivation and practical applicability of Contribution (2) unconvincing.

C. In my view, Contribution (3) is the most compelling. However, the paper fails to acknowledge a substantial body of prior work on the properties of the Jaccard distance. The omission of these citations raises concerns—whether intentional or not—regarding the completeness of the literature review. For instance, the triangle inequality of the Jaccard (Tanimoto) distance was proven in:

> A.H. Lipkus, A proof of the triangle inequality for the Tanimoto distance, J. Math. Chem., 26(1), 1999, pp. 263–265.

Related work on dissimilarity measures includes:

> A. Gragera and V. Suppakitpaisarn, Relaxed triangle inequality ratio of the Sørensen–Dice and Tversky indexes, Theoretical Computer Science, 718, 2018, pp. 37–45.

> T. Itoh and Y. Takei, On Aggregating Two Metrics with Relaxed Triangle Inequalities by the Weighted Harmonic Mean, IEICE Transactions on Fundamentals, Vol. E101-A, No. 9, pp. 1404–1411.

D. I was unable to locate the formal statement of Theorem 4.2 in either the main text or the appendix. Given that Theorem 4.2 appears to be a central contribution of the paper, its omission suggests a lack of thorough proofreading prior to submission.

Minor Comment

Line 863: The inequality should be $J(x, z) \leq$, not $J(x, z) \leftarrow$.

QUESTIONS:
I do not have any question for the authors.

LIMITATIONS:
Please refer to the section "Strengths and Weaknesses".
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper lists properties (also called axioms) that uniquely characterize Hamming and Jaccard distances (popular measures for comparing binary vectors). They also propose modifications of these distances called geometric/quadratic Hamming/Jaccad. The authors conduct experiments on synthetic and real data aimed at comparing distance measures.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
**Strengths:**
- There is a theoretical analysis that describes two popular measures by their properties. This analysis helps to better understand and compare the measures.
- The task of empirically comparing measures is, in general, non-trivial, and the authors propose several experiments aiming at such a comparison.
- The paper is, in general, well written.

**Weaknesses (see details below):**
- Some relevant literature is missing.
- Popular measures are known to have certain biases, which are not discussed.
- There are many other measures that can be analyzed, some of them having better theoretical properties.
- Not all properties are well-motivated.

**Detailed feedback**

**Related literature on evaluating classification performance measures.** 
Distance measures for binary vectors are essentially performance measures for binary classification. In particular, given the anonymity axiom, distance measures are expressed in terms of the entries of the 2x2 confusion matrix. There is a paper by Gösgens et al. (2021) that proposes an axiomatic approach for classification performance measures (binary and multiclass). This work, while being relevant and analyzing some of the listed properties, is not discussed in the current paper.

*M. Gösgens et al. “Good classification measures and how to find them.” NeurIPS 2021.*

**Proposed axioms.** 
Some of the proposed axioms were discussed in Gösgens et al. (2021), while others are not well motivated. Thus, using the term “axiom” instead of, e.g., “properties” seems to be misleading.
- Anonymity: basically says that a measure is defined by the four entries of the confusion matrix.
- Scaling: similar to *scale-invariance* in Gösgens et al. (2021)
- Independent symmetry: this property is not motivated, it is not clear why one should expect it to hold.
- Zero-one symmetry: also not motivated.
- Convergence: the chosen speed of convergence is not motivated.
- Triangle inequality: somehow similar to *distance* in Gösgens et al. (2021), but without other distance axioms.
- Normalization: similar to *minimal agreement* in Gösgens et al. (2021)

**Constant baseline property.**
An important and long-known shortcoming of both Hamming (*accuracy* for binary classification) and Jaccard is that they are biased, i.e., may have an undesirable behavior when classes are imbalanced. This property is known to be critical for reliable evaluation, but it is not discussed in this paper. Also, there are measures like Matthews correlation coefficient and Cohen’s Kappa that are known to be unbiased.

**Other measures.**
There are many measures that can be applied to the task at hand, but only two are discussed in this paper.

**Experiments.**
I like that there are various experiments allowing one to better understand and compare the measures. I have a couple of questions regarding the experiment in Section 6.2:
- Can it be the case that the obtained results depend on a particular clustering algorithm chosen?
- I see that the results illustrate a clear difference between the measures, but why would one want to cluster a dataset that is uniformly generated and thus is not supposed to contain a cluster structure?

**Minor:**
- Line 64: $z_i = y_i$ should probably be $z_i = y_{i-n}$
- Line 238: should it be $f$ instead of $d$ here?
- Line 317: “As mentioned introduction”

QUESTIONS:
Please, see the section Strengths And Weaknesses.

LIMITATIONS:
yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The authors have developed an axiomatic characterization of the Hamming and Jaccard distances, capturing key properties of each measure. Previous research dealing with axiomatic approaches have not dealt with Hamming distances, as shown in this current paper. They show that these characterizations differ by a single axiom: Zero-One Symmetry for Hamming vs Add Zero for Jaccard. A shared disadvantage is that these distances satisfy the Independent Symmetry, which may be undesirable in certain applications. They address this by applying a set of generalized mean measures. Examples of how these distances work are shown using election data.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Clarity: The paper is well structured
Significance, and Originality: The paper addresses the gap of better understanding operational aspects of Jaccard and Hamming distances in the context of binary vectors.

QUESTIONS:
* Line 42: ‘Indeed, both distances satisfy Independent Symmetry, which in practice implies that the vectors of different saturation will always be far from 43 each other.’ What does the term ‘saturation’mean?

* In the statements of Theorem 3.9 and 4.3 - what does the phrase ‘except for this one’ refer to? 

* In Section 6.1: Can a profile always only have a single ordering in place for the proposition to hold? What happens if a set of rows are ordered one way and another set not ordered at all, under the same r. 

* For clustering (section 6.2) and the election results, how would the results change for a different radii setting. Also, how did the authors arrive at 0.015, 0.15, 0.33 as radii settings in the examples shown?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Reject

COMMENT:
Based on a comprehensive evaluation of the paper and reviewers' feedback, I recommend ​rejecting​ this submission. Below is the detailed justification structured around critical flaws that undermine its contributions:

1. Reviewer SpSd confirms the authors themselves acknowledge Contribution (1) (verifying axioms for Hamming/Jaccard) is trivial. Contribution (3) (axioms for Jaccard) replicates known properties, e.g., the triangle inequality was proven in Lipiak (1999) and Graigeria et al. (2018). The work ignores Gössgens et al. (2021), which established an axiomatic framework for classification metrics applicable to binary vectors. This omission misrepresents the novelty of the axiomatic approach (Reviewer FAAB).

2. Key axioms (Independent Symmetry, Zero-One Symmetry, Convergence speed) lack practical or theoretical motivation. Reviewer FAAB notes these are not established as universally desirable properties, yet the paper frames them as foundational without justification. Both distances have well-documented biases(e.g., poor performance under class imbalance). Ignoring this—while alternatives like Matthews Correlation Coefficient address it—invalidates claims of a "comprehensive analysis" (Reviewer FAAB).

This paper attempts to axiomatize well-studied distances but delivers trivial verification of known properties, omits foundational literature, and selects axioms arbitrarily. The rebuttal offered superficial fixes (e.g., moving citations) but failed to motivate axioms, address biases, or prove core theorems. Given the ​lack of novelty, weak motivation, and unresolved execution gaps, the paper does not meet NeurIPS standards. I endorse rejection.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
